top_stories.label: Top Stories
top_stories.ai_summary: Mid-afternoon mood: tense and expensive. President Trump is back from Beijing with handshakes but no real deals, and oil is sitting above $109 a barrel after ships were seized and sunk in the Strait of Hormuz over the weekend. Wall Street closed Friday in the red on inflation and bond-yield jitters. Cuba is literally going dark — diesel has run out and Havana is enduring up to 22-hour blackouts — while Russia restarted its drone war on Ukraine the moment the US-brokered truce ran out. The through-line of today's news is a world running on fumes: fragile ceasefires, fragile supply chains, and very little slack.

stories.headline: Trump leaves Beijing without breakthroughs; Taiwan arms package still on the table
stories.summary: President Trump wrapped up a two-day summit with Xi Jinping, returning home with a tentative 200-jet Boeing order but no major agreements on Iran, trade or Taiwan. Trump told reporters he has not yet decided whether to approve the pending US arms sale to Taiwan, calling it a 'very good negotiating chip'. The two sides agreed a vague 'strategic stability' framework for the next three years.

stories.headline: Strait of Hormuz tensions spike: ship seized off UAE, another sunk near Oman
stories.summary: A commercial vessel anchored off the United Arab Emirates was seized on May 15 and taken toward Iran, while a second ship was attacked and sank near the coast of Oman. The incidents are the sharpest escalation since the fragile US–Iran ceasefire signed in April and pushed Brent crude up more than 3% to $109 a barrel.

stories.headline: Cuba completely out of diesel and fuel oil; Havana blackouts hit 22 hours a day
stories.summary: Cuba's energy minister Vicente de la O Levy said on May 14 the country has 'absolutely no fuel, oil, and absolutely no diesel'. Many Havana neighbourhoods are without power for 20–22 hours a day, and protests have broken out in the western areas around the capital. Neither Venezuela nor Mexico has shipped fuel to the island since Trump's January executive order threatening tariffs on any country supplying Cuba.

stories.headline: Wall Street tumbles as oil surges and Treasury yields hit 1-year high
stories.summary: The S&P 500 fell 1.24% to 7,408.50, the Dow lost 537 points (-1.07%), and the Nasdaq dropped 1.54% on Friday. The 10-year Treasury yield jumped to 4.55% — the highest in a year — as rising oil prices from the Hormuz crisis reignited inflation fears. Boeing slid 2.8% after a China order came in smaller than the 500 jets markets were hoping for.

stories.headline: Putin hints Ukraine war 'coming to an end' — Kyiv unconvinced as 200 drones strike overnight
stories.summary: President Vladimir Putin suggested his country's war on Ukraine may be 'coming to an end' and offered to meet Zelenskyy in a third country. Hours after a US-brokered three-day truce expired, Russia launched more than 200 drones at Ukraine. Zelenskyy responded that 'Russia has no intention of ending this war' and that Kyiv is bracing for more attacks.

stories.headline: Pope Leo XIV: AI and high-tech weapons risk leading the world into 'spiral of annihilation'
stories.summary: Pope Leo XIV used a Vatican address to denounce the investment surge into artificial intelligence and high-tech weaponry, warning it is dragging the world toward a 'spiral of annihilation'. He called for renewed peace efforts in the Middle East and Ukraine and named AI-controlled weapons systems as a specific moral and security threat.

stories.headline: Drones now cause 80% of civilian deaths in Sudan's fourth year of war
stories.summary: The UN says drones have caused more than 80% of civilian deaths in Sudan in the first four months of 2026, killing at least 880 people. A May 8 strike on Al Quz in South Kordofan reportedly killed 26 civilians. SAF and RSF delegates have begun pre-negotiation talks in Jeddah, but fighting in Darfur and Kordofan continues to escalate.

singapore.label: Singapore
singapore.ai_summary: Wet afternoons remain the headline story locally — NEA's fortnightly outlook keeps short, sharp thundery showers in the forecast through the rest of May, and PUB issued flood advisories around Jurong West this morning. On the wallet side, COE Cat A premiums climbed for a sixth straight bidding round to S$124,790, and dengue cases for the year are down 66% versus 2025 — though MOH is still pushing the Mozzie Wipeout routine as peak season starts. Police also issued an appeal for a missing 84-year-old in Tampines.

stories.headline: PUB flash-flood advisory: motorists told to avoid parts of Jurong West after morning deluge
stories.summary: PUB urged motorists to avoid affected stretches in Jurong West on the morning of May 16 after a sudden heavy downpour overwhelmed drainage. The agency continues to monitor 210 sensors across canals and drains as Sumatra squall season runs through the rest of May.

stories.headline: NEA: thundery showers expected on most afternoons through end-May
stories.summary: NEA's fortnightly outlook says inter-monsoon conditions will dominate the second half of May, with short-duration thundery showers on most afternoons and Sumatra squalls bringing gusty pre-dawn rain on some days. Most of Singapore has already recorded above-average rainfall this month, with 114.6mm in one afternoon near Tampines on May 8.

stories.headline: COE premiums climb for sixth straight round — Cat A hits S$124,790
stories.summary: The first COE bidding exercise of May 2026 closed with premiums rising across every category. Cat A (smaller cars) ended at S$124,790, Cat B (larger cars) at S$126,236, Cat C (commercial vehicles) at S$87,479 and Cat E (open) at S$127,700. LTA noted the upcoming Car Expo could push premiums higher and urged buyers to bid prudently. The next exercise opens May 18.

stories.headline: Dengue cases down 66% year-on-year, but peak season is starting
stories.summary: NEA says Singapore has logged just over 600 dengue cases between January 1 and May 15, 2026 — a 66% drop compared with the same period in 2025. MOH and NEA are still pushing the B-L-O-C-K Mozzie Wipeout routine as peak season (May–October) begins, especially in identified active clusters. The updated National Dengue Clinical Guideline (Mar 2026) is in use across hospitals.

stories.headline: Housing: enhanced CPF grant of up to S$40,000 for first-time buyers; EC policy under review
stories.summary: Singapore is widening housing support for first-time buyers this year, headlined by the Enhanced CPF Housing Grant of up to S$40,000. It stacks with the Single Singapore Citizen Grant (S$25,000), Proximity Housing Grant (S$15,000) and an Additional Housing Grant of up to S$10,000 for special cases. National Development Minister Chee Hong Tat has also confirmed a review of Executive Condominium policy amid affordability concerns.

stories.headline: Lower public-transport fare caps and wider subsidy tiers kick in
stories.summary: New transport measures in 2026 introduce a lower daily fare cap so commuters never spend above a fixed amount per day, and expand subsidies into middle-income households that previously did not qualify. The Land Transport and Related Matters Bill — covering active mobility, ERP 2.0 and vehicle safety — is also moving through Parliament.

stories.headline: Police appeal for missing 84-year-old man last seen in Tampines
stories.summary: Singapore Police Force on May 16 issued an appeal for public information on an 84-year-old man last seen in Tampines on May 15. Separately, police also issued appeals for two teenage girls missing in unrelated cases. Anyone with information is asked to call 1800-255-0000.

wars.label: Wars & Conflicts
wars.ai_summary: Three theatres are simmering at different temperatures this afternoon. Russia–Ukraine is back to full drone war after a brief US-brokered truce expired — Putin is talking peace, Kyiv isn't buying it. The US–Iran ceasefire is the most fragile: ships are being seized and sunk in the Strait of Hormuz, pushing Brent above $109. Israel–Lebanon's ceasefire just got a 45-day extension, but Israeli strikes are still hitting southern Lebanon daily — with more than 2,800 killed and over a million displaced since the war began. Sudan, often forgotten, is now in its fourth year, with drones causing 80% of civilian deaths.

theatres.name: Russia – Ukraine War
theatres.status: Active — drone war resumed after 3-day truce expired

timeline.event: Russia launches full-scale invasion of Ukraine.
War grinds into stalemate; Western military aid continues amid political tensions in Washington.
Russia launches over 8,000 drones in a single month — the highest monthly total since the invasion began.
Russia and Ukraine declare competing ceasefires around Victory Day.
US President Trump announces a 72-hour truce overlapping with Russia's Victory Day; both sides honour it briefly.
Putin says the war 'may be coming to an end' and offers to meet Zelenskyy in a third country.
Truce expires; Russia fires 200+ drones overnight at Ukraine. Ukraine retaliates by striking gas facilities in Russia's Orenburg region, 1,500km from the border.
Russia loses net 12 sq mi of territory in the past week — net 45 sq mi since mid-April.
Zelenskyy says Russia 'has no intention of ending this war' and warns of more attacks; Kyiv apartment-block strike killed 24 including three children.

theatres.name: US/Israel – Iran War & Strait of Hormuz Crisis

timeline.event: US and Israel launch joint air war against Iran. Opening strike kills Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei; Iran retaliates with hundreds of missiles and thousands of drones across the region.
Iran declares the Strait of Hormuz 'closed' and threatens to attack any ship attempting to transit.
US and Iran agree on a ceasefire that includes Israel after roughly five weeks of fighting.
Ceasefire formally takes effect. Conflict shifts to brinkmanship over Strait access and Tehran's counter-demands (sovereignty over the Strait, war damages, asset releases, sanctions relief).
US launches 'Operation Project Freedom' to escort merchant ships out of the Gulf; US and Iranian forces trade shots in the Strait.
Trump pauses Project Freedom citing 'great progress' toward an agreement; Pakistan PM Sharif credited as mediator.
Commercial vessel seized off UAE and taken toward Iran; second ship attacked and sunk near Oman. Brent crude jumps 3.37% to $109.30.

theatres.name: Israel – Lebanon & Gaza
theatres.status: Ceasefire extended 45 days, but daily strikes continue

timeline.event: Conflict between Israel and Hezbollah intensifies in southern Lebanon; ground operations and air strikes displace hundreds of thousands.
US President Trump announces a Lebanon–Israel ceasefire to halt fighting with Hezbollah.
Gaza's deadliest month of 2026: 140+ reported fatalities; violent events up 35% vs March; Israel kills the head of Hamas military intelligence.
Israeli air strikes on Lebanon kill 41 people in 24 hours; nearly 40 wounded near Tyre.
Board of Peace envoy Mladenov: Gaza ceasefire 'hinges on Hamas disarmament'; Hamas says disarmament is 'not negotiable'.
US confirms productive Israel–Lebanon talks in Washington; second 45-day ceasefire extension agreed, with next round set for June 2–3.
Israeli strike targets Haddad, who officials say was undermining Trump's Gaza plan. Cumulative Lebanon toll: 2,800+ killed, 1M+ displaced (>20% of Lebanon's population).

theatres.name: Sudan Civil War
theatres.status: Year 4 — drone war escalating, pre-negotiation talks in Jeddah

timeline.event: Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) begin civil war in Khartoum.
SAF retakes Khartoum from RSF; RSF retains control of Darfur and large parts of the west.
UN fact-finding mission identifies 'hallmarks of genocide' in Darfur.
Drones cause 80%+ of civilian deaths in Sudan in the first four months of 2026 — at least 880 killed.
Drone strikes on Al Quz, South Kordofan and near El Obeid kill 26 civilians.
30M Sudanese now need humanitarian aid; SAF and RSF delegates begin pre-negotiation talks in Jeddah, brokered by Saudi Arabia and the US.

hantavirus.label: Hantavirus Outbreak
hantavirus.ai_summary: The MV Hondius cruise-ship cluster has stabilised but is still serious: 11 cases, three deaths as of May 13, with Andes hantavirus — the only hantavirus known to spread human-to-human — confirmed in two of the deaths. Passengers were repatriated from Tenerife to seven countries last week, and contact tracing is active. WHO classifies the global risk as low and has said publicly this is 'not the start of another COVID'. The US CDC has it at a level-3 emergency response. No new cases have been reported since the May 13 update.

stories.headline: MV Hondius cluster: 11 cases, three deaths confirmed by WHO update
stories.summary: As of May 13, WHO and ECDC report 11 cases (8 confirmed, 1 inconclusive, 2 probable) tied to the MV Hondius, with three deaths — two confirmed as caused by Andes virus and one probable. The first case is believed to have been infected on land in South America before boarding the ship.

stories.headline: Ship reached Tenerife May 10; passengers repatriated to seven countries
stories.summary: After approval from Spanish health authorities, the MV Hondius sailed for Tenerife with 147 people aboard on May 6 and arrived May 10. Passengers were disembarked and flown home to six European countries and Canada under quarantine protocols, with international contact tracing ongoing.

stories.headline: WHO: outbreak is 'not the start of another COVID'
stories.summary: WHO has emphasised that the global public-health risk is low. Andes virus only spreads through close, sustained, human-to-human contact and has historically been contained to close-contact settings. However, the case fatality rate of hantavirus pulmonary syndrome remains high — 20–40% historically.

stories.headline: US CDC classifies the response as 'Level 3' emergency
stories.summary: The US CDC has classified the MV Hondius outbreak as a level-3 emergency response — its mid-tier activation level — and issued a HAN advisory to clinicians. CDC, WHO, ECDC and Spanish health authorities are coordinating epidemiological investigations with Argentina and Chile to trace the original land-based source.

cybersecurity.label: CyberSecurity
cybersecurity.ai_summary: It's a busy patch cycle. Cisco's Catalyst SD-WAN controller is being hit by a CVSS-10 zero-day with a CISA federal deadline tomorrow (May 17). Microsoft's May Patch Tuesday plugged 120 bugs, and CISA flagged a Linux kernel root-escalation flaw (CVE-2026-31431, 'Fragnasia') as actively exploited. The headline for defenders is human, not technical: Google's threat-intel team caught the first confirmed AI-generated zero-day — a 2FA bypass aimed at mass exploitation — and stopped it before it shipped. A new Windows BitLocker bypass dubbed 'YellowKey' has a public PoC and no patch. Patch Cisco and Windows tonight; assume AI-built exploits are the new normal.

stories.headline: Critical Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN auth-bypass exploited in zero-day attacks
stories.summary: Cisco warned of a critical authentication-bypass flaw in Catalyst SD-WAN Controllers that lets unauthenticated attackers gain admin privileges. Active exploitation has been observed. CISA added it to the KEV catalog on May 14 with a federal patching deadline of May 17, 2026 — tomorrow.

stories.headline: Microsoft May 2026 Patch Tuesday: 120 fixes including critical Windows GDI and SharePoint RCEs
stories.summary: Microsoft's May Patch Tuesday addressed 120 flaws — 17 rated Critical, 14 of them RCE. Notable: CVE-2026-35421 (Windows GDI RCE via malicious EMF file in Paint) and CVE-2026-40365 (SharePoint Server RCE). Microsoft says no zero-days were disclosed in this cycle.

stories.headline: CISA adds Linux kernel root-escalation bug 'Fragnasia' to KEV
stories.summary: A local privilege-escalation flaw in the Linux kernel — codenamed Fragnasia — lets an unprivileged user obtain root. CISA added it to the KEV catalog on May 1 with a federal remediation deadline of May 15, 2026. Fixes are available in kernel versions 6.18.22, 6.19.12 and 7.0.

stories.headline: Google catches first AI-generated zero-day: a 2FA bypass aimed at mass exploitation
stories.summary: Google's Threat Intelligence Group disrupted a planned mass-exploitation campaign that used an AI-generated Python exploit to bypass 2FA on a widely deployed open-source admin tool. Tell-tale signs included a hallucinated CVSS score, abundant 'textbook' docstrings, and clean LLM-style structure. Google says it is the first confirmed AI-built zero-day used in the wild.

stories.headline: Windows BitLocker zero-day 'YellowKey' with public PoC bypasses encrypted drives
stories.summary: A pair of Windows zero-days — codenamed YellowKey (BitLocker bypass via WinRE) and GreenPlasma (CTFMON privilege escalation) — were disclosed with a working proof-of-concept by researcher 'Chaotic Eclipse'. The BitLocker flaw lets a local attacker access protected drives on Windows 11 and Server 2022/2025. Microsoft has NOT shipped a patch — May Patch Tuesday did not include a fix. Mitigation: enable a BitLocker PIN at boot.

stories.headline: Fortinet FortiClient EMS zero-day under active mass exploitation
stories.summary: Fortinet released an emergency patch for a critical pre-auth API access bypass in FortiClient EMS (versions 7.4.5–7.4.6). watchTowr observed in-the-wild exploitation on March 31 ahead of the April 4 advisory. CISA added the bug to KEV on April 6; Singapore CSA also issued an alert. A compromised EMS lets attackers push malicious endpoint policies and pivot laterally.

stories.headline: Instructure Canvas breach: ShinyHunters claim 275M student records stolen
stories.summary: Instructure, maker of the Canvas LMS, confirmed a cyber incident affecting its cloud environment. ShinyHunters claims it stole roughly 275 million records belonging to students, teachers, and staff across 8,809 institutions worldwide — described as the largest education-sector breach on record.

stories.headline: Foxconn hit by ransomware; attackers claim 11 million customer files stolen
stories.summary: Foxconn, the contract manufacturer behind devices for Apple, Google, Nvidia, Dell, and Intel, was hit by a cyberattack at its North America facilities. The attackers claim to have stolen more than 11 million files, including confidential information from major Foxconn customers.
